source.title: Rivaroxaban with or without Aspirin in Stable Cardiovascular Disease

extraction.summary: The COMPASS trial evaluated whether rivaroxaban (2.5 mg twice daily) plus aspirin or rivaroxaban (5 mg twice daily) alone would be more effective than aspirin alone for secondary cardiovascular prevention in 27,395 patients with stable atherosclerotic vascular disease. The combination of rivaroxaban plus aspirin reduced the primary endpoint of cardiovascular death, stroke, or MI by 24% (HR 0.76, 95% CI 0.66-0.86, p<0.001) compared to aspirin alone, but increased major bleeding by 70% (HR 1.70, 95% CI 1.40-2.05, p<0.001). Rivaroxaban alone did not significantly reduce cardiovascular events but increased bleeding risk.

population.age_criteria: Mean age 68.2 years; patients with coronary artery disease <65 years required documentation of atherosclerosis in ≥2 vascular beds or ≥2 additional risk factors
population.risk_criteria: Stable atherosclerotic vascular disease with coronary artery disease (90.6%), peripheral arterial disease (27.3%), or both
population.disease_definition: Coronary artery disease or peripheral arterial disease with additional risk factors including smoking, diabetes, GFR <60 ml/min, heart failure, or nonlacunar ischemic stroke ≥1 month earlier
population.exclusions: High bleeding risk, recent stroke or previous hemorrhagic/lacunar stroke, severe heart failure, advanced kidney disease (GFR <15 ml/min), dual antiplatelet therapy, anticoagulation, poor prognosis noncardiovascular conditions
population.severity_staging: Stable disease with 61.8-62.7% having previous MI, 21.4-21.7% with heart failure, 37.5-38.1% with diabetes

key_thresholds.context: CV death/stroke/MI in rivaroxaban+aspirin vs aspirin alone

key_thresholds.parameter: Primary composite outcome (CV death, stroke, MI)
key_thresholds.context: Rivaroxaban+aspirin vs aspirin alone, HR 0.76 (95% CI 0.66-0.86), P<0.001
Rivaroxaban+aspirin vs aspirin alone, HR 0.80 (95% CI 0.70-0.91), P<0.001
Rivaroxaban+aspirin vs aspirin alone, HR 0.51 (95% CI 0.38-0.68), P<0.001
rivaroxaban plus aspirin vs aspirin alone

recommendations.recommendation: Rivaroxaban 2.5 mg twice daily plus aspirin 100 mg once daily reduces cardiovascular events but increases major bleeding compared to aspirin alone
recommendations.notes: Trial stopped early for efficacy after mean 23 months follow-up

recommendations.recommendation: Rivaroxaban 5 mg twice daily alone does not significantly reduce cardiovascular outcomes compared to aspirin alone and increases bleeding risk
recommendations.notes: No significant difference in primary endpoint with rivaroxaban monotherapy

recommendations.recommendation: Net clinical benefit favors rivaroxaban plus aspirin over aspirin alone in stable CAD/PAD patients
recommendations.notes: Balance of efficacy and safety outcomes shows overall benefit

recommendations.recommendation: Rivaroxaban 2.5mg twice daily plus aspirin reduces major adverse cardiovascular events in stable atherosclerotic vascular disease
recommendations.notes: 24% relative risk reduction but 70% increase in major bleeding

recommendations.recommendation: Net clinical benefit favors rivaroxaban plus aspirin combination therapy
recommendations.notes: Balances cardiovascular benefit against bleeding risk

recommendations.recommendation: Rivaroxaban alone (5mg twice daily) not recommended
recommendations.notes: No significant reduction in primary outcome but increased bleeding risk

recommendations.recommendation: Effects consistent across subgroups including age, sex, geography, and cardiovascular risk factors
In patients with stable atherosclerotic vascular disease, rivaroxaban plus aspirin reduces cardiovascular events but increases bleeding risk
recommendations.notes: Risk-benefit assessment needed for individual patients

recommendations.recommendation: Rivaroxaban alone did not show cardiovascular benefit over aspirin and increased bleeding risk
recommendations.notes: Not recommended as monotherapy over aspirin

medications.indication: Secondary prevention in stable atherosclerotic vascular disease
Secondary prevention in stable atherosclerotic vascular disease

red_flags: Major bleeding increased 70% with rivaroxaban plus aspirin
Gastrointestinal bleeding was the predominant bleeding type
No significant difference in intracranial or fatal bleeding between groups
16.5-17.0% permanent discontinuation rates across treatment arms
High bleeding risk was an exclusion criterion
Gastrointestinal bleeding was the predominant type of major bleeding
Intracranial bleeding occurred more frequently with rivaroxaban combinations
Serious adverse events occurred in 7.9% (rivaroxaban+aspirin), 7.7% (rivaroxaban alone), and 7.3% (aspirin alone)
Significantly increased major bleeding risk with rivaroxaban (both doses)
Risk-benefit consideration required for individual patients
extraction.follow_up: Mean follow-up of 23 months (maximum 47 months). Participants seen at 1 month, 6 months, then every 6 months. Trial stopped early for superiority of rivaroxaban plus aspirin after first interim analysis.
extraction.escalation_path: Results apply to stable atherosclerotic vascular disease patients at low-moderate bleeding risk. Individual risk-benefit assessment needed given increased bleeding with combination therapy. Separate analyses of coronary and peripheral arterial disease cohorts reported separately. Ongoing pantoprazole vs placebo comparison continues for gastroprotection evaluation.
